examples.explanation: Step 1: Test vertex 0. Remove 0, graph becomes edges: 1-2, 1-3. Start DFS from 1, visits 1,2,3. All vertices (1,2,3) visited in one component. components=1, not articulation. Step 2: Test vertex 1. Remove 1, graph becomes edges: 0-2. Start DFS from 0, visits 0,2. Vertex 3 not visited. Start DFS from 3, visits 3. components=2 > 1, articulation point. Step 3: Test vertex 2. Remove 2, graph becomes edges: 0-1, 1-3. Start DFS from 0, visits 0,1,3. All vertices visited. components=1. Step 4: Test vertex 3. Remove 3, graph becomes edges: 0-1, 0-2, 1-2. Start DFS from 0, visits 0,1,2. components=1. Result: [1].

Step 1: Test vertex 0. Remove 0, graph becomes edge: 1-2. Start DFS from 1, visits 1,2. components=1. Step 2: Test vertex 1. Remove 1, graph becomes no edges. Start DFS from 0, visits 0. Vertex 2 not visited. Start DFS from 2, visits 2. components=2 > 1, articulation point. Step 3: Test vertex 2. Remove 2, graph becomes edge: 0-1. Start DFS from 0, visits 0,1. components=1. Result: [1].

approaches.name: Brute Force O(V*(V+E))
approaches.summary: For each vertex, temporarily remove it from the graph and check if the number of connected components increases using DFS/BFS. If removal increases components, it's an articulation point. This is conceptually simple but inefficient for large graphs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Articulation Points Detection
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of brute force approach to find all articulation points in an undirected graph.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution using the brute force approach. The intuition is straightforward: an articulation point is a vertex whose removal increases the number of connected components in the graph. We systematically test each vertex by temporarily removing it and counting components. The algorithm works by iterating through all vertices. For each vertex v, we create a modified graph without v and all its incident edges. We then perform DFS/BFS from an arbitrary remaining vertex to count reachable vertices. If not all vertices (excluding v) are reachable, we have multiple components, making v an articulation point. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize an empty list for articulation points. 2. For each vertex v in the graph: a. Create a visited array marking v as visited (effectively removed). b. Start DFS from the next unvisited vertex (if any). c. Count how many DFS calls are needed to visit all vertices except v. d. If count > 1, add v to articulation points. 3. Return the list. Time complexity is O(V*(V+E)) because for each of V vertices, we run DFS/BFS which takes O(V+E). Space complexity is O(V+E) for the graph representation and visited array. This connects to the full problem by implementing the complete brute force logic. Common mistakes include not properly handling disconnected graphs initially, forgetting to skip the removed vertex in DFS, and incorrect component counting. Real code pattern: def articulation_points_brute(graph): n = len(graph); aps = []; for v in range(n): visited = [False]*n; visited[v] = True; components = 0; for i in range(n): if not visited[i]: components += 1; dfs(i, graph, visited); if components > 1: aps.append(v); return aps.

examples.trace: Step 1: Test vertex 0. Remove 0, graph becomes edges: 1-2, 1-3. Start DFS from 1, visits 1,2,3. All vertices (1,2,3) visited in one component. components=1, not articulation. Step 2: Test vertex 1. Remove 1, graph becomes edges: 0-2. Start DFS from 0, visits 0,2. Vertex 3 not visited. Start DFS from 3, visits 3. components=2 > 1, articulation point. Step 3: Test vertex 2. Remove 2, graph becomes edges: 0-1, 1-3. Start DFS from 0, visits 0,1,3. All vertices visited. components=1. Step 4: Test vertex 3. Remove 3, graph becomes edges: 0-1, 0-2, 1-2. Start DFS from 0, visits 0,1,2. components=1. Result: [1].

Step 1: Test vertex 0. Remove 0, graph becomes edge: 1-2. Start DFS from 1, visits 1,2. components=1. Step 2: Test vertex 1. Remove 1, graph becomes no edges. Start DFS from 0, visits 0. Vertex 2 not visited. Start DFS from 2, visits 2. components=2 > 1, articulation point. Step 3: Test vertex 2. Remove 2, graph becomes edge: 0-1. Start DFS from 0, visits 0,1. components=1. Result: [1].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Component Counting After Vertex Removal
ladders.desc: Count connected components in a graph after removing a specific vertex.
ladders.explanation: This is the most important helper function: given a graph and a vertex to remove, count how many connected components exist in the remaining graph. The intuition is that if removal splits the graph into multiple disconnected parts, that vertex was critical. The algorithm: 1. Mark the removed vertex as visited (so DFS skips it). 2. Initialize component count to 0. 3. For each vertex in the graph: if not visited, increment count and run DFS from that vertex to mark all reachable vertices. 4. Return the count. Time complexity is O(V+E) for one DFS/BFS traversal. Space complexity is O(V) for visited array. This feeds into L0 by being called for each vertex. Common mistakes: not marking removed vertex as visited initially, incorrect loop for starting DFS, and miscounting components in disconnected graphs. Real code pattern: def count_components_without_vertex(graph, removed_v): n = len(graph); visited = [False]*n; visited[removed_v] = True; components = 0; for v in range(n): if not visited[v]: components += 1; dfs(v, graph, visited); return components.

examples.trace: Step 1: Mark vertex 1 as visited. Step 2: Start from vertex 0 (not visited), DFS visits 0,2. Step 3: Vertex 3 not visited, start DFS from 3, visits 3. Step 4: All vertices visited, components counted: from 0 and 3 gives 2 components.
Step 1: Mark vertex 0 as visited. Step 2: Start from vertex 1 (not visited), DFS visits 1,2. Step 3: All vertices visited, components counted: 1 component.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Vertex Removal Impact on Connectivity
ladders.desc: Determine if removing a vertex increases the number of connected components.
ladders.explanation: The central insight: an articulation point is identified by checking if its removal disconnects the graph. This involves comparing connectivity before and after removal. The intuition is that in a connected graph, all vertices are reachable from any start; removal of a critical vertex breaks this property. The algorithm: 1. Count components in original graph (should be 1 if connected). 2. Temporarily remove vertex v (ignore it and its edges). 3. Count components in modified graph. 4. If count increases, v is an articulation point. Time complexity for one vertex is O(V+E) for counting. Space is O(V+E). This is the core technique used in L1. Common mistakes: not handling initially disconnected graphs, comparing absolute counts instead of increase, and incorrect modification of graph. Real code pattern: def is_articulation_brute(graph, v): original_components = count_components(graph); modified_components = count_components_without_vertex(graph, v); return modified_components > original_components.

examples.trace: Step 1: Original graph components: DFS from 0 visits all vertices, count=1. Step 2: Remove vertex 1: graph becomes edges: 0-2 and vertex 3 isolated. DFS from 0 visits 0,2; DFS from 3 visits 3; count=2. Step 3: 2 > 1, so true.
Step 1: Original components: DFS from 0 visits all, count=1. Step 2: Remove vertex 0: graph becomes edge: 1-2. DFS from 1 visits 1,2; count=1. Step 3: 1 > 1 is false.

ladders.title: Building Block — Counting Connected Components in a Graph
ladders.desc: Determine the number of connected components using DFS/BFS.
ladders.explanation: An intermediate concept: a connected component is a subgraph where any two vertices are connected by a path. Counting components is fundamental to graph analysis. The intuition is that each DFS/BFS traversal from an unvisited vertex explores one entire component. The algorithm: 1. Initialize visited array all false. 2. Initialize count = 0. 3. For each vertex v: if not visited, increment count and run DFS/BFS from v to mark all reachable vertices as visited. 4. Return count. Time complexity is O(V+E) as each vertex and edge is processed once. Space complexity is O(V) for visited array and recursion stack. This feeds into L2 by providing the component count. Common mistakes: not resetting visited array, incorrect traversal logic, and missing vertices in disconnected graphs. Real code pattern: def count_components(graph): n = len(graph); visited = [False]*n; components = 0; for v in range(n): if not visited[v]: components += 1; dfs(v, graph, visited); return components.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start DFS from vertex 0, mark visited: 0,1,2,3. Step 2: All vertices visited in one traversal, so components=1.
Step 1: Start DFS from vertex 0, mark visited: 0,1. Step 2: Vertex 2 not visited, start DFS from 2, mark visited: 2,3. Step 3: Two traversals, components=2.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Graph Traversal (DFS/BFS)
ladders.desc: Visit all vertices reachable from a starting vertex using Depth-First Search or Breadth-First Search.
ladders.explanation: A fundamental operation: traversal explores all vertices connected to a start vertex. The intuition is to systematically visit neighbors, marking each to avoid repeats. For DFS: use recursion or a stack; for BFS: use a queue. Algorithm (DFS): 1. Mark current vertex as visited. 2. For each neighbor not visited, recursively call DFS. Time complexity for traversing a component is O(V+E) in adjacency list. Space is O(V) for visited and recursion stack. This feeds into L3 by being the core of component counting. Common mistakes: infinite recursion if graph has cycles and not marking visited, stack overflow in deep graphs, and incorrect neighbor iteration. Real code pattern: def dfs(v, graph, visited): visited[v] = True; for neighbor in graph[v]: if not visited[neighbor]: dfs(neighbor, graph, visited).

examples.trace: Step 1: Visit 0, mark visited. Step 2: Neighbors of 0: 1 and 2. Visit 1 (not visited), mark. Step 3: Neighbors of 1: 0 and 2. 0 visited, visit 2 (not visited), mark. Step 4: Neighbors of 2: 0 and 1, both visited. Done.
Step 1: Visit 0, mark. Step 2: Neighbor of 0: 1. Visit 1, mark. Step 3: Neighbor of 1: 2. Visit 2, mark. Step 4: Neighbor of 2: none. Done.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Representation and Basic Operations
ladders.desc: Understand graphs as vertices and edges, and perform basic operations like accessing neighbors.
ladders.explanation: The simplest prerequisite: a graph is a set of vertices connected by edges. For articulation points, we use undirected graphs. Common representation: adjacency list (list of lists) where graph[i] contains neighbors of vertex i. Basic operations include initializing a graph, adding edges, and iterating through neighbors. The intuition is that graphs model relationships, and we need efficient ways to store and access them. Step-by-step: 1. Define graph as a list of lists with V empty lists. 2. For each edge (u,v), add v to graph[u] and u to graph[v] (undirected). 3. To access neighbors of vertex i, use graph[i]. Time complexity for adding edges is O(1) per edge; space is O(V+E). This feeds into L4 by providing the data structure for traversal. Common mistakes: using directed graph for undirected problem, index errors, and incorrect initialization. Real code pattern: V = 4; graph = [[] for _ in range(V)]; graph[0].append(1); graph[1].append(0); # for edge 0-1.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize graph with 3 empty lists: [[], [], []]. Step 2: Add edge 0-1: append 1 to graph[0], append 0 to graph[1]. Graph becomes [[1], [0], []]. Step 3: Add edge 1-2: append 2 to graph[1], append 1 to graph[2]. Graph becomes [[1], [0,2], [1]].
Step 1: Initialize graph with 2 empty lists: [[], []]. Step 2: Add edge 0-1: append 1 to graph[0], append 0 to graph[1]. Graph becomes [[1], [0]].

approaches.name: Tarjan's DFS O(V+E)
approaches.summary: Perform a DFS traversal while maintaining discovery times and low values for each vertex. A vertex is an articulation point if: (1) it's root with >1 child, or (2) for non-root, any child's low value >= discovery time of vertex. This is the classic optimal solution.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find All Articulation Points in an Undirected Graph
ladders.desc: Implement Tarjan's DFS algorithm to identify all articulation points in an undirected graph.
ladders.explanation: Articulation points (or cut vertices) are vertices whose removal increases the number of connected components in a graph. Tarjan's algorithm efficiently finds these points using a single DFS traversal. The intuition is that during DFS, we track two values for each vertex: discovery time (when it's first visited) and low value (the earliest discovery time reachable from that vertex through its descendants). A vertex is an articulation point if: (1) It's the DFS root and has more than one child in the DFS tree, meaning removing it disconnects those subtrees. (2) For non-root vertices, any child's low value is greater than or equal to the vertex's discovery time, indicating no back edge from that child's subtree to an ancestor of the vertex, so removal disconnects that subtree. The algorithm performs DFS from each unvisited vertex, recursively computing low values by considering back edges (edges to already visited ancestors). Time complexity is O(V+E) as each vertex and edge is processed once. Space complexity is O(V) for arrays and recursion stack. This builds on L1 by integrating the DFS traversal with articulation point detection logic. Common mistakes include not handling disconnected graphs (need to start DFS from each unvisited vertex), incorrectly updating low values (must use min of discovery times from back edges), and misidentifying root articulation points (check children count in DFS tree, not original graph degree).

examples.trace: Step 1: Start DFS from vertex 0 (time=0). disc[0]=0, low[0]=0. Visit neighbor 1 (time=1). disc[1]=1, low[1]=1. From 1, visit neighbor 2 (time=2). disc[2]=2, low[2]=2. From 2, visit neighbor 3 (time=3). disc[3]=3, low[3]=3. Vertex 3 has no unvisited neighbors, return to 2. Update low[2]=min(low[2], low[3])=min(2,3)=2. Check articulation: for child 3, low[3]=3 >= disc[2]=2 → 2 is articulation point. Return to 1. Update low[1]=min(low[1], low[2])=min(1,2)=1. Check articulation: for child 2, low[2]=2 >= disc[1]=1 → 1 is not articulation (since 2 has back edge to 0? Actually low[2]=2, disc[1]=1, condition holds but need to verify: from 2, back edge to 0 (already visited) updates low[2]=min(2, disc[0])=0, so low[2]=0 < disc[1]=1, so 1 is not articulation. Let's correct trace: After visiting 2 from 1, from 2 explore neighbor 0 (already visited, not parent), so low[2]=min(low[2], disc[0])=min(2,0)=0. Then from 2 visit 3 as before. So low[2]=0. Then back to 1: low[1]=min(1,0)=0. Check: for child 2, low[2]=0 < disc[1]=1 → 1 not articulation. Return to 0. Update low[0]=min(low[0], low[1])=min(0,0)=0. Root 0 has children: from 0 visited 1 and 2? Actually from 0, first neighbor 1, then from 1 visited 2, so in DFS tree, children of 0 is only 1 (since 2 visited via 1). So root has 1 child → not articulation. DFS from vertex 3 already visited. Result: only vertex 2 is articulation point.

Step 1: Start DFS from vertex 0 (time=0). disc[0]=0, low[0]=0. Visit neighbor 1 (time=1). disc[1]=1, low[1]=1. From 1, visit neighbor 2 (time=2). disc[2]=2, low[2]=2. From 2, visit neighbor 3 (time=3). disc[3]=3, low[3]=3. From 3, visit neighbor 4 (time=4). disc[4]=4, low[4]=4. Vertex 4 has no unvisited neighbors, return to 3. Update low[3]=min(low[3], low[4])=min(3,4)=3. Check articulation: for child 4, low[4]=4 >= disc[3]=3 → 3 is articulation point. Return to 2. Update low[2]=min(low[2], low[3])=min(2,3)=2. Check articulation: for child 3, low[3]=3 >= disc[2]=2 → 2 is articulation point. Return to 1. Update low[1]=min(low[1], low[2])=min(1,2)=1. Check articulation: for child 2, low[2]=2 >= disc[1]=1 → 1 is articulation point. Return to 0. Update low[0]=min(low[0], low[1])=min(0,1)=0. Root 0 has 1 child → not articulation. Result: vertices 1,2,3 are articulation points.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Traversal with Discovery and Low Values
ladders.desc: Perform a DFS that computes discovery times and low values for each vertex in an undirected graph.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of Tarjan's algorithm, where we traverse the graph using DFS while maintaining two arrays: disc[] for discovery times (when a vertex is first visited) and low[] for the earliest discovery time reachable from that vertex. The intuition is to explore the graph deeply, tracking how vertices are connected through back edges (edges to already visited ancestors). The algorithm starts from a source vertex, assigns an increasing time to each newly visited vertex, and recursively visits neighbors. For each vertex u, low[u] is initialized to disc[u]. During backtracking, low[u] is updated as the minimum of its current value and the low values of its children (for tree edges) and the discovery times of already visited neighbors (for back edges). This helps identify cycles and connectivity. Time complexity is O(V+E) as it's a standard DFS with extra O(1) updates per edge. Space complexity is O(V) for arrays and recursion stack. This feeds into L0 by providing the computed disc and low arrays, which are used to check articulation point conditions. Common mistakes include not handling multiple DFS calls for disconnected graphs, incorrectly updating low for back edges (use disc[v] not low[v]), and not passing parent to avoid revisiting the immediate parent in undirected graphs. Real code pattern uses recursion with a time counter.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start at vertex 0, time=0, disc[0]=0, low[0]=0. Visit neighbor 1 (unvisited), time=1, disc[1]=1, low[1]=1. From 1, visit neighbor 2 (unvisited), time=2, disc[2]=2, low[2]=2. From 2, visit neighbor 3 (unvisited), time=3, disc[3]=3, low[3]=3. Vertex 3 has no unvisited neighbors, return to 2. Update low[2]=min(low[2], low[3])=min(2,3)=2. From 2, neighbor 0 is visited and not parent (parent is 1), so low[2]=min(2, disc[0])=min(2,0)=0. From 2, neighbor 1 is visited and is parent, skip. Return to 1. Update low[1]=min(low[1], low[2])=min(1,0)=0. From 1, neighbor 0 is visited and not parent? Parent of 1 is 0, so skip. From 1, neighbor 2 is visited and not parent, but already processed. Return to 0. Update low[0]=min(low[0], low[1])=min(0,0)=0. From 0, neighbor 2 is visited and not parent, so low[0]=min(0, disc[2])=min(0,2)=0. Result: disc=[0,1,2,3], low=[0,0,0,3].

Step 1: Start at vertex 0, time=0, disc[0]=0, low[0]=0. Visit neighbor 1, time=1, disc[1]=1, low[1]=1. From 1, visit neighbor 2, time=2, disc[2]=2, low[2]=2. From 2, visit neighbor 3, time=3, disc[3]=3, low[3]=3. From 3, visit neighbor 4, time=4, disc[4]=4, low[4]=4. Vertex 4 has no unvisited neighbors, return to 3. Update low[3]=min(low[3], low[4])=min(3,4)=3. No back edges from 3. Return to 2. Update low[2]=min(low[2], low[3])=min(2,3)=2. No back edges. Return to 1. Update low[1]=min(low[1], low[2])=min(1,2)=1. No back edges. Return to 0. Update low[0]=min(low[0], low[1])=min(0,1)=0. No back edges. Result: disc=[0,1,2,3,4], low=[0,1,2,3,4].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Identifying Articulation Points Using Low and Discovery Times
ladders.desc: Determine if a vertex is an articulation point based on low values and discovery times from DFS.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the logical conditions that define articulation points using the disc and low arrays computed during DFS. The intuition is that a vertex u is critical if removing it disconnects the graph. For the DFS root, this happens if it has more than one child in the DFS tree, meaning its removal separates those subtrees since there are no edges between them (otherwise they'd be in one subtree). For non-root vertices, u is an articulation point if there exists a child v such that low[v] >= disc[u]. This condition means that the subtree rooted at v has no back edge to an ancestor of u, so removing u would disconnect v's subtree from the rest of the graph. If low[v] < disc[u], then there's a back edge from v's subtree to an ancestor of u, preserving connectivity. The algorithm checks these conditions during DFS backtracking: when returning from a child v to u, if low[v] >= disc[u] and u is not root, mark u as articulation. For the root, count children; if >1, mark as articulation. Time complexity is O(1) per vertex check, integrated into DFS. Space is O(1) extra. This builds on L1 by adding the articulation checks to the DFS traversal. Common mistakes include checking the condition incorrectly (e.g., using low[u] instead of low[v]), not handling root separately, and mis counting children (only count in DFS tree, not all neighbors).

examples.input: Vertex u=2, disc[u]=2, low values: for child 3, low[3]=3; for child 1 (via back edge?), but in DFS tree, children of 2 might be 3. Condition: low[3]=3 >= disc[2]=2 → u is articulation.
examples.trace: Given vertex u=2 with discovery time 2. During DFS, suppose u has a child v=3 in the DFS tree with low[3]=3. Check condition: low[3] >= disc[2]? 3 >= 2 is true. Since u is not root (assume root is 0), mark u as articulation point. If low[3] were 1, then 1 >= 2 is false, so u would not be articulation.

examples.input: Root vertex u=0, children in DFS tree: from 0 visited 1 and 2 as separate children? Actually if graph is tree, children count=2.
examples.trace: For root u=0, we need to count how many children it has in the DFS tree. During DFS from root 0, suppose it first visits neighbor 1, then later from root visits neighbor 2 (if 2 not visited via 1). So children count=2. Since 2 > 1, root is articulation point. If root had only 1 child (e.g., in a chain), then not articulation.

ladders.title: Building Block — Computing Low Values in DFS for Undirected Graphs
ladders.desc: Calculate the low value for each vertex during DFS, considering back edges to ancestors.
ladders.explanation: The low value of a vertex u is the earliest discovery time reachable from u by traversing zero or more tree edges followed by at most one back edge. It helps identify cycles and connectivity. The intuition is that low[u] represents how high up in the DFS tree u can reach via its descendants. During DFS, when visiting a neighbor v of u: if v is unvisited (tree edge), recursively compute low[v], then update low[u] = min(low[u], low[v]). If v is already visited and v is not the parent of u (back edge), update low[u] = min(low[u], disc[v]) (not low[v], because back edge connects directly to v's discovery time). This update ensures low[u] captures the minimum discovery time in the cycle. The algorithm initializes low[u] = disc[u] when u is first visited. Time complexity is O(1) per edge update, within DFS O(V+E). Space is O(V) for low array. This feeds into L2 by providing low values needed for articulation checks. Common mistakes include updating low with low[v] for back edges (should use disc[v]), not skipping the parent in undirected graphs (to avoid immediate backtrack), and incorrect initialization.

examples.input: Vertex u=2 with disc[2]=2, neighbors: v=3 (unvisited), v=0 (visited, disc[0]=0, not parent), parent=1
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize low[2]=disc[2]=2. Process neighbor v=3 (unvisited). Recursively compute low[3] (say disc[3]=3, low[3]=3). After returning, update low[2]=min(low[2], low[3])=min(2,3)=2. Process neighbor v=0 (visited, disc[0]=0, and v is not parent since parent is 1). This is a back edge. Update low[2]=min(low[2], disc[0])=min(2,0)=0. Skip neighbor v=1 (parent). Result: low[2]=0.

examples.input: Vertex u=1 with disc[1]=1, neighbors: v=0 (visited, disc[0]=0, parent? if parent is 0, then skip), v=2 (unvisited)
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize low[1]=1. Process neighbor v=0: if parent is 0 (since DFS came from 0 to 1), skip because it's the immediate parent in undirected graph. Process neighbor v=2 (unvisited). Compute low[2] recursively. Suppose from 2, there's a back edge to 0, so low[2]=0. Update low[1]=min(low[1], low[2])=min(1,0)=0. Result: low[1]=0.

testCases.input: u disc=1, neighbors: v disc=0 (visited, not parent)
u disc=2, neighbors: v unvisited with low=3
u disc=3, neighbors: v unvisited with low=2
u disc=4, neighbors: v visited disc=1 (not parent), w unvisited low=5
u disc=5, neighbors: parent disc=4, v visited disc=3 (not parent)
u disc=6, neighbors: v unvisited low=7, w visited disc=5 (not parent)
u disc=7, neighbors: v visited disc=6 (parent)
u disc=8, neighbors: v unvisited low=8
u disc=9, neighbors: v visited disc=8 (not parent), w unvisited low=10

ladders.title: Basic Operation — DFS Traversal on an Undirected Graph
ladders.desc: Perform a depth-first search on an undirected graph, marking visited vertices.
ladders.explanation: DFS is a fundamental graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. For undirected graphs, we start from a source vertex, mark it visited, and recursively visit all adjacent unvisited vertices. The intuition is to systematically explore connected components. The algorithm uses a visited array to avoid cycles and repeated visits. Pseudocode: function dfs(u): visited[u]=true; for each neighbor v of u: if not visited[v]: dfs(v). Time complexity is O(V+E) as each vertex and edge is processed once. Space complexity is O(V) for visited array and recursion stack. This feeds into L3 by providing the traversal framework where we add disc and low computations. Common mistakes include not handling disconnected graphs (need to call dfs on each unvisited vertex), infinite recursion in graphs with cycles (prevented by visited array), and using BFS instead of DFS for Tarjan's algorithm.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start at vertex 0, mark visited. Neighbors: 1 and 2. Visit 1 (unvisited), mark visited. From 1, neighbors: 0 and 2. 0 is visited, skip. Visit 2 (unvisited), mark visited. From 2, neighbors: 0,1,3. 0 and 1 visited, skip. Visit 3 (unvisited), mark visited. 3 has no neighbors. Backtrack to 2, then 1, then 0. DFS completes. Visited set: {0,1,2,3}.

Step 1: Start at 0, mark visited. Neighbor: 1. Visit 1, mark visited. From 1, neighbors: 0 and 2. 0 visited, skip. Visit 2, mark visited. 2 has no unvisited neighbors. Backtrack to 1, then 0. Visited set: {0,1,2}.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Representation and Basic Terminology
ladders.desc: Understand undirected graphs, vertices, edges, and adjacency lists.
ladders.explanation: A graph is a collection of vertices (nodes) and edges connecting pairs of vertices. In an undirected graph, edges have no direction; an edge (u,v) is the same as (v,u). Key terminology: vertex (point), edge (link), adjacency (two vertices connected by an edge), degree (number of edges incident to a vertex), path (sequence of vertices where each adjacent pair is connected by an edge), cycle (path that starts and ends at the same vertex), connected component (maximal set of vertices where there is a path between any two). Graphs can be represented as adjacency lists (array of lists, where each list contains neighbors of a vertex) or adjacency matrices. For Tarjan's algorithm, adjacency list is efficient. Intuition: graphs model networks like social connections or road maps. This foundation is needed for L4 to perform DFS. Common mistakes include confusing directed and undirected graphs, incorrect adjacency list construction (e.g., not adding both directions for undirected edges), and misunderstanding connectivity.

examples.trace: Step 1: List vertices: 0,1,2. For edge (0,1): add 1 to adjacency list of 0, and add 0 to adjacency list of 1. For edge (1,2): add 2 to list of 1, and add 1 to list of 2. Result: vertex 0 neighbors: [1]; vertex 1 neighbors: [0,2]; vertex 2 neighbors: [1].

examples.input: Graph with vertices A,B,C and edges (A,B), (B,C), (C,A)
examples.trace: Step 1: Vertices A,B,C. Edge (A,B): add B to A's list, add A to B's list. Edge (B,C): add C to B's list, add B to C's list. Edge (C,A): add A to C's list, add C to A's list. Result: A:[B,C], B:[A,C], C:[A,B].
